content.decision_question: Should Hungary pursue ambitious structural redesign of the categorical/gatekeeping architecture despite disruption risk, or favor incremental, piloted change that preserves stability but may leave core bottlenecks unresolved longer?
content.evidence_boundary: Comparative evidence can show what disruption or persistence-of-variation outcomes occurred elsewhere under similar reforms, informing risk estimates. It cannot determine how much disruption Hungarian stakeholders should be willing to accept in exchange for structural improvement, since that is a value judgment about acceptable transition costs.
content.tension: Transitioning to a non-categorical, tiered model (TP-live-t3) targets a documented bottleneck (sole nationwide gatekeeper, multi-month waiting lists) and could reduce diagnosis-dependent access barriers, but comparative evidence from Poland shows rapid, non-consensual structural reform can produce severe implementation crises and stakeholder backlash, while Finland's experience shows persistent regional variation survives even successful reform.
content.title: Structural gatekeeping redesign versus disruption risk
